app.kicker: TU/e · Library & Information Services · AI Enablement Program
app.lede: Seven dimensions, four levels, one figure — a self-reflection instrument for LIS, rendered as an interactive publication plate.

landing.heading: Where does AI literacy stand, here, today?
landing.body: Twenty-eight short questions across seven dimensions produce a nuanced profile of your own AI literacy. Nothing leaves your browser unless you choose to export it.
landing.whatsNew: New in v3 — refreshed question set, updated heptagon diagram, refined scoring, individual self-reflection only.

notes.time.title: Time
notes.time.body: About 15 minutes. Honest beats complete — pick the option that sounds most like your day-to-day.
notes.privacy.title: Privacy
notes.privacy.body: No accounts, no tracking, no server. Your answers live in this browser tab until you choose to download them. If you share your export, individual results are intended to be used in aggregated form to identify training needs.
notes.export.title: What you get out
notes.export.body: A JSON file you can keep or share, and a typeset PNG of the heptagon plate for reports and slides.

intro.why.title: Why are we mapping AI literacy in LIS?
intro.why.body.1: Artificial Intelligence is increasingly part of how we support education, research, and operations at TU/e. As Library and Information Services, we already engage with AI in many ways: advising staff and students, selecting tools, designing services, interpreting legal and ethical implications, and supporting responsible use.
intro.why.body.2: At the same time, AI affects roles and services differently. Not everyone needs the same level of expertise, and nobody is expected to be an expert in everything.
intro.why.body.3: This is why LIS is using the AI Literacy Heptagon: as a shared reflection and development tool, not as a test.
intro.what.title: What is the AI Literacy Heptagon?
intro.what.body.1: The AI Literacy Heptagon describes AI literacy as seven interconnected dimensions: technical understanding of AI, practical use of AI tools, critical evaluation of AI outputs, integration of AI into workflows and services, legal and regulatory awareness, ethical reasoning, and understanding of societal impact.
intro.what.body.2: For each dimension, four levels are used: Unaware → Beginner → Intermediate → Expert. These levels describe how you relate to AI in practice, not how "good" you are.
intro.reasons.title: Why are we asking you to reflect on this?
intro.reasons.1.title: 1. To understand where we are now
intro.reasons.1.body: LIS already has a lot of knowledge and experience — but it is unevenly distributed across roles, teams, and services. A shared overview helps us see strengths, gaps, and overlaps.
intro.reasons.2.title: 2. To clarify where we need to be
intro.reasons.2.body: Different LIS product areas require different AI literacy profiles. Some roles need stronger legal and ethical expertise; others need stronger integration or application skills. Mapping this helps us be intentional rather than reactive.
intro.reasons.3.title: 3. To support learning and development
intro.reasons.3.body: The results will be used to guide training and upskilling efforts, support team discussions around AI in our roles, and inform strategic choices around AI in LIS. This is about growth, not judgement.
intro.notATest.title: What this is not
intro.notATest.body: To be very clear, this exercise is not a performance evaluation, not mandatory, not a test of intelligence or competence, not a way to rank individuals or teams, and not an expectation that everyone must reach "expert" level. There is no "ideal" personal profile.
intro.approach.title: How to approach the questions
intro.approach.body.1: Answer honestly, based on your current situation. Choose the option that best reflects your day-to-day practice, not your aspirations.
intro.approach.body.2: It is completely normal — and expected — to score differently across dimensions. Many people are Intermediate in one area and Beginner in another. That's exactly what this tool is designed to show.

consent.label: I understand that my individual answers stay in my browser unless I choose to export them, and that exported answers may be used — in aggregated form — to identify training and development needs across LIS.
consent.required: Please check the box above before continuing.

citation.label: Based on
citation.body: Hackl, V., Müller, A. E., & Sailer, M. (2026). The AI Literacy Heptagon: A Structured Approach to AI Literacy in Higher Education.
citation.note: The seven dimensions and four Bloom-aligned levels are adopted directly from the paper's Heptagon framework. The 28 sub-questions were authored by the TU/e LIS AI Literacy Team.

scope.individual.title: About you
scope.individual.body: Optional. The name is kept only on the exported file; the rest helps place your profile in the org for training-needs analysis.
scope.field.name: Name
scope.field.team.other: Other — not part of a team

assess.heading: {{dimension}}
assess.sub.prompt: Answer all four questions for this dimension, then continue.
assess.evidence.label: Evidence or notes (optional)

results.heading: Your profile
results.body: Below is the heptagon you just described, with each dimension scored as the mean of its four sub-questions. Dimensions that sit inside the terracotta baseline band are below the Beginner level LIS expects from every staff member.

pilotFeedback.heading: Help us improve this tool
pilotFeedback.body: This is a pilot version. Two quick questions, both optional.
pilotFeedback.savingNotice: Your feedback is only sent when you download the JSON file below — fill it in before exporting, otherwise it will be lost.
pilotFeedback.clarity.label: Were the questions clear?
pilotFeedback.notes.label: Which questions felt unclear or could be improved?

aggregator.lede: Drop in the JSON profiles your team has exported. Everything is computed in your browser — no profile ever leaves this tab.
aggregator.drop.hint: One or more heptagon JSON exports (schema v2.0 or v3.0). Files that don't match the schema are flagged and skipped.
aggregator.children.empty: No profiles at this level yet.
aggregator.drilldownHint: Click a card to drill down.
aggregator.validation.heading: Files skipped
aggregator.compare.hint: Click up to three cards to overlay them.
aggregator.compare.tooFew: Select at least two cards to compare.

margin.reading.title: Reading the plate
margin.reading.body: Seven axes, one per dimension. Four concentric rings: Unaware, Beginner, Intermediate, Expert. The terracotta band marks the Beginner baseline expected of every LIS member.
margin.profile.title: The profile
margin.profile.body: The red polygon is your current self-reflection, each dimension scored as the mean of four sub-questions.
margin.interaction.title: Interaction
margin.interaction.body: Hover any vertex for the dimension's mean and sub-scores. Export as JSON or a typeset PNG plate.

technical.purpose: Understanding how AI systems function and where their limits are.
technical.description: This dimension involves understanding the fundamental principles and mechanisms of AI, including algorithms, data processing, and computational concepts. It is important because a basic grasp of how AI functions is essential for accurately evaluating its capabilities, limitations, and ethical implications.

application.purpose: Effective, reflective, and purposeful use of AI tools.
application.description: This refers to the ability to effectively utilize AI technologies to solve practical problems and improve workflows across diverse contexts. It is important for ensuring efficient and contextually appropriate AI deployment, such as using prompt engineering or AI assistants to enhance professional tasks.

integration.purpose: Embedding AI meaningfully into LIS services and workflows.
integration.description: This dimension focuses on incorporating and adapting AI technologies into existing digital environments and organizational workflows. It is important for navigating professional transformations, identifying appropriate integration points, and managing the transition between human and AI-driven processes.

legal.purpose: Safe, compliant AI use in education and research support.
legal.description: This involves understanding both AI-specific legislation (like the EU AI Act) and technology-neutral frameworks (like GDPR and copyright). It is vital for navigating legal responsibilities and ensuring that the development and use of AI remain compliant with evolving regional and global regulations. Concrete risks include exposing personal data — a GDPR breach, with consequences for the people involved and for TU/e — or sharing confidential TU/e information through an external AI tool.

socialImpact.purpose: Understanding AI's effect on society, education, and LIS roles.
socialImpact.description: This dimension covers the ability to comprehend the multifaceted effects of AI on societal structures and human interactions. It is important for recognizing the long-term consequences of AI on diverse populations and understanding the interplay between technological advancement and socio-economic systems.

ethical.description: This encompasses the ability to identify and address ethical implications at the individual, societal, and environmental levels. It is important for safeguarding values such as privacy, fairness, and human dignity, while also considering the environmental impact of AI resource consumption.

criticalThinking.description: This is the capacity to analyze, evaluate, and critically assess AI systems, their outputs, and their suitability for specific use cases. It is crucial for identifying risks like "hallucinations" or algorithmic bias, preventing the uncritical acceptance of AI-generated information.

TKS1.prompt: How would you describe your understanding of how AI systems (e.g. generative AI) produce outputs?

anchors.unaware: I do not know how AI systems work internally
anchors.beginner: I know AI is trained on data and recognises patterns
anchors.intermediate: I understand concepts like models, probabilities, and hallucinations
anchors.expert: I can compare and explain different AI approaches and implications

TKS2.prompt: How well do you understand the limitations of current AI systems?

anchors.unaware: I am not aware of specific limitations
anchors.beginner: I know AI can be wrong or biased
anchors.intermediate: I can explain why AI makes errors and when outputs are unreliable
anchors.expert: I can analyse risks and failure modes across contexts

TKS3.prompt: How confidently can you explain AI concepts to students or colleagues?

anchors.intermediate: I adapt explanations and correct misconceptions
anchors.expert: I teach or design learning materials about AI

TKS4.prompt: How comfortable are you discussing AI using correct terminology?

anchors.unaware: I am unfamiliar with AI terminology
anchors.beginner: I recognise common terms (e.g. model, training data)
anchors.intermediate: I use terminology accurately in discussions
anchors.expert: I critically evaluate and refine how AI is explained

AP1.prompt: How do you currently use AI tools in your work?

anchors.unaware: I do not use AI tools
anchors.beginner: I experiment occasionally for simple tasks
anchors.intermediate: I regularly use AI tools for work tasks
anchors.expert: I strategically design AI-supported workflows

AP2.prompt: How do you improve the quality of AI outputs?

anchors.intermediate: I iteratively refine prompts and inputs
anchors.expert: I develop and share effective prompting strategies

AP3.prompt: How well can you judge whether AI is appropriate for a task?

anchors.beginner: I rely on trial and error
anchors.intermediate: I assess risks, benefits, and task fit
anchors.expert: I define criteria for appropriate AI use

AP4.prompt: How do you respond when AI output is incorrect or misleading?

anchors.unaware: I don't notice or question it
anchors.intermediate: I systematically verify and correct outputs
anchors.expert: I teach others how to critically evaluate AI output

CTA1.prompt: How critically do you evaluate AI-generated information?

anchors.intermediate: I verify sources, logic, and assumptions
anchors.expert: I critically audit AI reasoning

CTA2.prompt: How aware are you of risks such as hallucinations or bias?

anchors.unaware: I am not aware of these risks
anchors.intermediate: I actively look for them
anchors.expert: I anticipate and explain them in context

CTA3.prompt: How do you decide whether to rely on AI output?

anchors.unaware: I accept or reject without analysis
anchors.intermediate: I apply explicit evaluation criteria
anchors.expert: I design evaluation frameworks

CTA4.prompt: How do you support others in critically using AI?

anchors.intermediate: I explain how to verify AI outputs
anchors.expert: I actively teach critical AI use

IS1.prompt: How do you integrate AI into existing workflows or services?

anchors.intermediate: I embed AI into workflows, or am in the process of doing so
anchors.expert: I redesign workflows around human–AI collaboration

IS2.prompt: How do you assess whether an AI tool fits a specific service or product?

anchors.intermediate: I evaluate relevance and impact
anchors.expert: I advise on strategic adoption

IS3.prompt: How do you respond to new AI developments relevant to your work?

anchors.beginner: I explore them out of curiosity
anchors.intermediate: I assess potential value
anchors.expert: I actively scan and advise on trends

IS4.prompt: How do you manage change caused by AI adoption?

anchors.intermediate: I plan integration and transitions
anchors.expert: I lead or guide change processes

LRK1.prompt: How familiar are you with AI-relevant legal frameworks (e.g. GDPR, copyright, EU AI Act)?

anchors.unaware: I am not familiar at all
anchors.beginner: I know they exist and know their content in general
anchors.intermediate: I apply them in concrete cases
anchors.expert: I interpret and explain legal implications

LRK2.prompt: How well can you identify legal risks in AI use?

anchors.intermediate: I identify common risks and safeguards
anchors.expert: I assess complex risk scenarios

LRK3.prompt: How do legal considerations affect your AI-related decisions?

anchors.intermediate: They consciously guide my choices
anchors.expert: I translate law into guidance or policy

LRK4.prompt: How do you support others with AI-related legal questions?

anchors.intermediate: I explain basic rules
anchors.expert: I advise or develop guidance materials

EAR1.prompt: How aware are you of ethical issues related to AI?

anchors.unaware: I am not aware of ethical issues
anchors.intermediate: I analyse ethical trade-offs
anchors.expert: I engage in ethical decision-making

EAR2.prompt: How do ethics influence your AI use?

anchors.intermediate: They guide concrete decisions
anchors.expert: I help define ethical standards

EAR3.prompt: How well can you identify ethical risks in AI use cases?

anchors.intermediate: I analyse context-specific risks
anchors.expert: I anticipate complex ethical dilemmas

EAR4.prompt: How do you address ethical questions raised by others?

anchors.intermediate: I discuss and reason through them
anchors.expert: I facilitate ethical reflection or governance

SIU1.prompt: How well do you understand AI's broader impact on society?

anchors.beginner: I know AI affects work and education
anchors.intermediate: I analyse impacts on equity and access
anchors.expert: I contribute to strategic discussions

SIU2.prompt: How do you consider AI's impact on students and researchers?

anchors.intermediate: I integrate it into advice or teaching
anchors.expert: I shape institutional approaches

SIU3.prompt: How aware are you of power, inclusion, and access issues related to AI?

anchors.intermediate: I actively consider them
anchors.expert: I advocate for responsible use

SIU4.prompt: How do societal considerations influence your professional role?

anchors.intermediate: They guide my actions
anchors.expert: They inform strategy or policy

heptagon.title: AI Literacy Profile
heptagon.desc.intro: A radar-like figure with seven axes. Profile levels:
